seoTitle: Lingxing IC Products - Precision ADC, DAC, Op-Amp Distributor
seoDescription: Browse Lingxing IC precision ADCs, DACs, operational amplifiers, and power management ICs. LiTong is your authorized distributor for high-performance analog solutions.

faqs.question: What product categories does Lingxing IC offer?
faqs.answer: Lingxing IC offers four main product categories: Precision ADCs (Analog-to-Digital Converters) featuring high-resolution sigma-delta and SAR architectures for accurate signal acquisition. Precision DACs (Digital-to-Analog Converters) providing high-accuracy voltage and current output for precise control. Operational Amplifiers including precision, high-speed, and low-power variants for signal conditioning. Power Management ICs such as voltage references and LDO regulators for stable power delivery. Each category is designed to meet demanding requirements in industrial automation, automotive, medical, and test equipment applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Review the product categories below to find the right Lingxing IC products for your application, or contact our FAE team for selection guidance.

faqs.question: How do I select the right Lingxing IC ADC for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right Lingxing IC ADC requires understanding your measurement requirements: Resolution - choose 16-24 bits for high precision, 12-16 bits for general purpose. Sampling rate - consider your signal bandwidth (Nyquist criterion). Architecture - sigma-delta for high precision DC/low-frequency, SAR for higher speed multiplexed applications. Input type - single-ended or differential based on signal source. Interface - SPI for higher speed, I2C for simpler wiring. Key specifications to evaluate: INL/DNL for linearity, SNR/SFDR for dynamic performance, offset/gain error for DC accuracy. For sensor interfaces, consider integrated PGA (Programmable Gain Amplifier). Our FAE team can provide detailed selection guidance based on your specific measurement needs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our selection guides in each category or contact LiTong FAE team for personalized ADC recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the difference between precision and general-purpose op-amps?
faqs.answer: Lingxing IC offers both precision and general-purpose operational amplifiers with different characteristics: Precision op-amps feature: Ultra-low offset voltage (<100μV, some <10μV). Low offset drift (<1μV/°C). High open-loop gain (>120dB). Low noise density. Higher cost, for demanding applications. General-purpose op-amps feature: Moderate offset voltage (1-10mV). Standard drift (5-20μV/°C). Adequate gain (>80dB). Lower cost, for standard applications. Use precision op-amps for: Sensor signal conditioning. Measurement equipment. Precision filters. Current sensing. Use general-purpose op-amps for: Buffering. Basic amplification. Non-critical filtering. Cost-sensitive designs. Lingxing IC provides options across both categories to meet various application needs and budgets.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use precision op-amps for high-accuracy applications, general-purpose for standard functions. Contact our FAE team for selection guidance.

faqs.question: Does Lingxing IC offer automotive-qualified analog ICs?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lingxing IC offers AEC-Q100 qualified analog ICs specifically designed for automotive applications. These automotive-grade products undergo rigorous qualification testing including high temperature operating life (HTOL), temperature cycling, electrostatic discharge (ESD) testing, and electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) validation to meet automotive reliability standards. Automotive product offerings include: Precision ADCs for battery monitoring, sensor interfaces, and control systems. DACs for motor control and actuator drive. Operational amplifiers for signal conditioning in harsh environments. Voltage references for precision measurements. All automotive products are qualified for operation over the -40°C to +125°C temperature range (Grade 1) and meet the quality standards required by Tier-1 automotive suppliers. These products are ideal for electric vehicles, ADAS, and automotive control systems.
faqs.decisionGuide: For automotive applications, look for AEC-Q100 qualified Lingxing IC parts. Contact our automotive FAE team for support.

faqs.question: What support does LiTong provide for Lingxing IC product selection?
faqs.answer: LiTong provides comprehensive support for Lingxing IC product selection including: Application engineering assistance from our experienced FAE team who can help with ADC/DAC/op-amp selection, signal chain design, schematic review, and PCB layout optimization. Reference designs with complete schematics, BOMs, and layout guidelines for typical applications. Evaluation boards for testing and validation of Lingxing IC solutions. Software drivers and code examples for various microcontroller platforms. Technical documentation including datasheets, application notes, and design guides. On-site technical support and training for high-volume projects. We can also coordinate directly with Lingxing IC's factory FAEs for complex technical challenges and provide competitive pricing for production volumes.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact LiTong technical support team for personalized assistance with Lingxing IC product selection and application design.

categories.name: Precision ADCs
categories.description: Lingxing IC precision ADCs deliver high-resolution analog-to-digital conversion with excellent accuracy and low noise. Available in both sigma-delta and SAR architectures, these ADCs offer resolutions from 12 to 24 bits, with features like integrated PGAs, low-drift references, and multiple interface options. Designed for industrial, automotive, and instrumentation applications requiring precise measurement of temperature, pressure, voltage, and other analog signals.

selectionGuide.title: Lingxing IC ADC Selection Guide
selectionGuide.description: Learn how to select the right Lingxing IC ADC for your precision measurement application.

faqs.question: What resolution options are available for Lingxing IC ADCs?
faqs.answer: Lingxing IC offers ADCs with various resolution options to meet different application needs: 12-14 bit ADCs for general-purpose applications where moderate precision is sufficient, such as basic monitoring and control systems. 16-18 bit ADCs for higher precision applications including industrial sensors and data acquisition. 20-24 bit ADCs for ultra-high precision applications like weigh scales, medical instrumentation, and scientific measurement. Higher resolution provides finer measurement steps and better dynamic range but typically comes with lower sampling rates. The effective resolution also depends on noise performance - a 24-bit ADC may have 20-22 effective bits in practice. When selecting resolution, consider: The smallest signal change you need to detect. The dynamic range of your input signal. The noise floor of your system. Cost and power consumption trade-offs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select resolution based on required precision. 16-18 bits for most industrial, 20-24 bits for high precision. Contact our FAE team for guidance.

faqs.question: How do I choose between Sigma-Delta and SAR ADCs?
faqs.answer: Lingxing IC offers both Sigma-Delta and SAR ADC architectures, each suited for different applications: Sigma-Delta ADCs are best for: High resolution (16-24 bits) applications. DC and low-frequency signals (<1kHz). Applications requiring excellent noise performance. When built-in filtering is beneficial. Examples: weigh scales, temperature sensors, precision voltage measurement. SAR ADCs are best for: Medium resolution (12-18 bits) applications. Higher sampling rates (up to several MSPS). Multiplexed multi-channel applications. Control loops requiring low latency. Examples: data acquisition, motor control, power monitoring. Key differences: Sigma-Delta has higher latency due to filtering, SAR has single-cycle conversion. Sigma-Delta excels at DC precision, SAR at AC performance. Sigma-Delta often includes PGA, SAR typically has wider input range. Consider your signal type, speed requirements, and channel count when selecting.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use Sigma-Delta for high precision DC, SAR for higher speed AC. Contact our FAE team for architecture selection.

faqs.question: What is INL and DNL in ADC specifications?
faqs.answer: INL (Integral Non-Linearity) and DNL (Differential Non-Linearity) are key specifications that describe ADC accuracy: INL measures the maximum deviation of the actual transfer function from an ideal straight line. It represents the overall linearity error across the full input range. Lower INL means better accuracy across the entire range. Typical values: ±1-2 LSB for good ADCs, ±0.5 LSB or better for precision ADCs. DNL measures the deviation of each code step from the ideal 1 LSB. It indicates missing codes or non-monotonic behavior. DNL must be less than ±1 LSB to guarantee no missing codes. Typical values: ±0.5-1 LSB for standard ADCs, ±0.3 LSB for precision ADCs. Why they matter: INL affects absolute accuracy and distortion. DNL affects resolution and monotonicity. Both impact measurement precision in data acquisition and sensor applications. Lingxing IC precision ADCs specify tight INL/DNL for demanding applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Look for INL/DNL < ±1 LSB for precision apps. Contact our FAE team for accuracy requirements analysis.

faqs.question: What interface options are available for Lingxing IC ADCs?
faqs.answer: Lingxing IC ADCs support multiple digital interfaces for flexible system integration: SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface): Most common interface for ADCs. Supports high clock rates (up to tens of MHz). Full-duplex communication. Simple protocol with low overhead. Ideal for high-speed data acquisition. I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit): Two-wire interface (SDA, SCL). Supports multiple devices on same bus. Lower speed than SPI (up to 3.4MHz). Good for space-constrained designs. Ideal for low-speed monitoring applications. Selection considerations: SPI for higher sampling rates and simpler software. I2C for reduced pin count and multi-device sharing. Some ADCs support both interfaces (pin-selectable). Check your MCU's available interfaces and speed requirements. Lingxing IC provides ADCs with both interface options to match different system architectures.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use SPI for higher speed, I2C for simpler wiring. Contact our FAE team for interface selection.

faqs.question: What is the significance of sampling rate in ADC selection?
faqs.answer: Sampling rate (or sample rate) is a critical ADC specification that determines how frequently the analog input is converted to digital: Nyquist criterion: Sample rate must be at least 2x the highest frequency in your signal. Practical designs typically use 5-10x oversampling for better reconstruction. Sampling rate vs application: 1-100 SPS: Temperature, pressure, slow sensors. 100-1000 SPS: General industrial monitoring. 1k-100k SPS: Audio, vibration, control systems. >100k SPS: High-speed data acquisition, communications. Trade-offs: Higher sampling rates consume more power. Higher rates may reduce effective resolution (noise). Faster ADCs typically cost more. Consider: The bandwidth of your input signal. Whether you need to capture transients. Power consumption constraints. Cost budget. Lingxing IC offers ADCs with sampling rates from a few SPS to several MSPS to address different application needs.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select sampling rate 5-10x your signal bandwidth. Contact our FAE team for sampling rate analysis.

products.name: 24-Bit Sigma-Delta ADC with PGA
products.shortDescription: Lingxing IC LX2448 24-bit precision ADC with integrated PGA for high-accuracy sensor measurement.
descriptionParagraphs: The LX2448 is a high-performance 24-bit sigma-delta ADC featuring an integrated programmable gain amplifier (PGA) for direct sensor interface.
This device delivers exceptional precision with low noise, excellent linearity, and flexible gain settings from 1 to 128.
The LX2448 is ideal for weigh scales, pressure sensors, temperature measurement, and other precision instrumentation applications.

faeReview.author: Michael Chen
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Precision Analog
faeReview.content: The LX2448 is an excellent choice for precision sensor measurement applications. The integrated PGA eliminates the need for external amplification in many designs, reducing component count and board space. I've used this ADC in multiple weigh scale designs and consistently achieve stable readings to 1/10,000 resolution or better. The noise performance is impressive - at PGA=128 and 10SPS, the 40nV RMS noise enables measurement of microvolt-level signals. The INL of ±10ppm ensures excellent linearity across the full input range. The flexible output data rate allows trading speed for noise performance based on application needs. The SPI interface is straightforward to implement, and the internal oscillator eliminates the need for an external crystal in most applications. For cost-sensitive precision measurement designs, the LX2448 offers performance comparable to more expensive alternatives.
faeReview.highlight: High-performance 24-bit ADC with integrated PGA for precision sensor applications

alternativeParts.reason: Higher sampling rate for faster applications with slightly lower PGA range.
alternativeParts.useCase: Use when higher speed is needed and maximum PGA gain is not required.

alternativeParts.reason: Higher speed SAR ADC for applications not requiring 24-bit precision.
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for data acquisition and control loops requiring higher speed.

companionParts.description: Precision voltage reference for ADC reference input
ARM Cortex-M4 MCU with SPI interface

faqs.question: How do I calculate the effective resolution of LX2448?
faqs.answer: The effective resolution (or ENOB - Effective Number Of Bits) of the LX2448 depends on PGA gain and output data rate: At PGA=1, 10SPS: ENOB ~ 21-22 bits (noise ~ 1.5μV RMS). At PGA=128, 10SPS: ENOB ~ 19-20 bits (noise ~ 40nV RMS). The effective resolution is always less than the 24-bit resolution due to noise. To calculate for your application: Determine the input-referred noise from datasheet (depends on PGA and ODR). Calculate the RMS noise in volts. Divide the full-scale range by noise to get SNR. Convert SNR to bits: ENOB = (SNR - 1.76) / 6.02. Example: PGA=128, ±39mV range, 40nV noise: SNR = 20×log(39mV/40nV) ≈ 120dB, ENOB ≈ (120-1.76)/6.02 ≈ 19.6 bits. The LX2448 provides excellent effective resolution for precision measurement applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Effective resolution depends on PGA and ODR. Contact our FAE team for noise analysis.

faqs.question: What is the best PGA gain setting for my sensor?
faqs.answer: Selecting the optimal PGA gain for the LX2448 involves matching the sensor output to the ADC input range: The LX2448 has a ±2.048V input range at PGA=1. With PGA gain G, the effective input range becomes ±2.048V/G. For example: PGA=1: ±2.048V range, suitable for large signals. PGA=32: ±64mV range, good for bridge sensors. PGA=128: ±16mV range, ideal for thermocouples. Selection guidelines: Calculate your sensor's maximum output voltage. Choose the highest PGA gain that accommodates this voltage with 20% headroom. Higher PGA reduces noise but limits input range. Consider startup transients and fault conditions. Example for load cell: 2mV/V sensitivity, 5V excitation = 10mV full scale. Use PGA=128 (±16mV range) for best noise performance. The PGA can be changed dynamically if different measurement ranges are needed.
faqs.decisionGuide: Match PGA gain to sensor output for optimal noise performance. Contact our FAE team for PGA selection.

faqs.question: How do I minimize noise in LX2448 measurements?
faqs.answer: Minimizing noise in LX2448 measurements requires attention to multiple aspects: Hardware design: Use clean, low-noise power supplies (LDO recommended). Add proper decoupling capacitors (0.1μF + 10μF) near the ADC. Keep analog input traces short and shielded if possible. Use differential inputs to reject common-mode noise. Implement proper grounding (star ground or ground plane). Software techniques: Use lower output data rates (more averaging = lower noise). Enable digital filter for additional noise reduction. Take multiple samples and average. Use appropriate PGA gain to maximize signal utilization. System considerations: Minimize digital switching near analog inputs. Keep ADC away from switching power supplies. Use external RC filters on inputs if needed. Consider temperature effects on noise. The LX2448's noise scales with √(ODR) - halving the data rate reduces noise by √2 (~30%).
faqs.decisionGuide: Optimize PCB layout, use lower ODR, and proper filtering. Contact our FAE team for noise optimization.

faqs.question: What reference voltage should I use with LX2448?
faqs.answer: The LX2448 supports both internal and external reference options: Internal reference: Convenient, no external components. 2.048V nominal output. 10-20ppm/°C drift (varies by grade). Suitable for many applications. External reference: Better accuracy and drift performance. Can use 2.5V, 3.0V, 4.096V, or 5V references. Recommended for high-precision applications. Selection considerations: For 0.1% accuracy or better: Use external precision reference. For wide temperature range: External reference with low drift (<5ppm/°C). For battery-powered apps: Internal reference saves power. Recommended external references: LX432 (2.5V, 3ppm/°C). LX4040 (4.096V, 5ppm/°C). Connect external reference to REFP/REFN pins. Bypass with 0.1μF capacitor close to pins. The reference voltage determines the ADC full-scale range and directly affects measurement accuracy.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use internal for convenience, external for higher precision. Contact our FAE team for reference selection.

faqs.question: How do I interface LX2448 with Arduino?
faqs.answer: Interfacing the LX2448 with Arduino is straightforward using SPI: Hardware connections: VDD to 3.3V or 5V (LX2448 accepts 2.7V-5.25V). GND to Arduino GND. SCLK to Arduino SCK (pin 13 on Uno). DIN to Arduino MOSI (pin 11). DOUT to Arduino MISO (pin 12). CS to Arduino pin 10 (any digital pin). DRDY to Arduino pin 2 (for interrupt, optional). Software: Use Arduino SPI library. Set SPI mode to MODE1 (CPOL=0, CPHA=1). Set clock speed to 1-2MHz (can go higher if needed). Send command bytes to configure ADC. Read conversion results. Basic read sequence: Pull CS low. Send RDATA command (0x01). Read 3 bytes (24-bit result). Pull CS high. Example libraries: Lingxing IC provides Arduino library. Community libraries available on GitHub. The LX2448 works well with Arduino for prototyping and low-speed data acquisition applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use SPI interface with MODE1. Contact our FAE team for Arduino example code.

faqs.question: What is the conversion latency of LX2448?
faqs.answer: The LX2448 conversion latency depends on the output data rate (ODR) setting: The LX2448 uses a sigma-delta architecture with digital filtering. Latency is primarily determined by the filter settling time. Typical latency values: 10 SPS: ~300-400ms latency (50/60Hz rejection). 80 SPS: ~40-50ms latency. 480 SPS: ~6-8ms latency. The latency is approximately 3-4 conversion cycles for full settling. Why latency matters: Control loops need low latency for stability. Data acquisition may need synchronized sampling. Real-time applications require predictable timing. Reducing latency: Use higher ODR settings. Accept higher noise for lower latency. Use single-cycle conversion mode if available. Note that lower latency (higher ODR) results in higher noise. For most measurement applications, the latency is not critical. For control applications, consider using the LX1632 SAR ADC instead.
faqs.decisionGuide: Latency 300ms at 10SPS, 6ms at 480SPS. Contact our FAE team for latency-critical applications.

products.name: 16-Bit SAR ADC with 8 Channels
products.shortDescription: Lingxing IC LX1632 16-bit 100kSPS SAR ADC with 8 input channels for data acquisition.
descriptionParagraphs: The LX1632 is a high-speed 16-bit SAR ADC featuring 8 input channels, 100kSPS sampling rate, and SPI interface for data acquisition applications.
This device offers low power consumption, excellent dynamic performance, and flexible input configuration for industrial and automotive systems.
The LX1632 is ideal for multi-channel data acquisition, motor control, power monitoring, and embedded measurement systems.

faeReview.author: David Wang
faeReview.title: Senior FAE - Data Acquisition
faeReview.content: The LX1632 is my go-to recommendation for multi-channel data acquisition applications requiring moderate speed and precision. The 100kSPS rate is sufficient for most industrial control and monitoring applications. The 8-channel multiplexer allows scanning multiple sensors with a single ADC, reducing system cost and complexity. The 16-bit resolution provides good dynamic range for most measurements. I particularly like the auto-scan mode which automatically sequences through selected channels, reducing MCU overhead. The SPI interface is easy to implement and the low power consumption makes it suitable for portable and battery-powered applications. The ±2 LSB INL ensures good linearity for control applications. For customers needing higher channel counts, multiple LX1632s can share the same SPI bus with different chip selects. The device is reliable and has proven itself in numerous industrial deployments.
faeReview.highlight: Versatile 16-bit SAR ADC with 8 channels for data acquisition

alternativeParts.reason: Higher sampling rate for faster applications.
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for higher speed data acquisition and control systems.

alternativeParts.reason: Higher resolution for precision measurement applications.
alternativeParts.useCase: Use for high-precision sensor measurement, not high speed.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the throughput for multi-channel sampling?
faqs.answer: Calculating multi-channel throughput for the LX1632: The total sampling rate is divided among active channels. Formula: Channel sampling rate = ADC sampling rate / Number of channels. Examples: 8 channels at 100kSPS = 12.5kSPS per channel. 4 channels at 100kSPS = 25kSPS per channel. 2 channels at 100kSPS = 50kSPS per channel. Timing considerations: Channel switching time: ~1μs. Conversion time: ~10μs at 100kSPS. Total cycle time: ~11μs per channel. For 8 channels: 88μs total = 11.36kSPS effective. To maximize throughput: Use auto-scan mode for continuous sampling. Minimize SPI communication overhead. Use burst mode if available. Consider using multiple ADCs for very high channel counts. The LX1632 provides good throughput for most multi-channel applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Divide 100kSPS by channel count. Contact our FAE team for multi-channel timing analysis.

faqs.question: What is the difference between single-ended and differential inputs?
faqs.answer: The LX1632 supports both single-ended and differential input configurations: Single-ended inputs: Signal measured relative to ground (AGND). 8 channels available (AIN0-AIN7). Simpler wiring and interface. More susceptible to ground noise. Suitable when: Ground is clean and stable. Cost and simplicity are priorities. Signal source is ground-referenced. Differential inputs: Signal measured between two inputs (AIN+ and AIN-). 4 channel pairs available. Rejects common-mode noise. Better noise immunity. Suitable when: Ground noise is present. Long cable runs are needed. High noise immunity required. The LX1632 can be configured for either mode via control register. Differential mode provides better performance in noisy environments but requires more wiring. For best performance in industrial environments, differential inputs are recommended.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use differential for noisy environments, single-ended for simple wiring. Contact our FAE team for input configuration.

faqs.question: How do I use the auto-scan mode of LX1632?
faqs.answer: The LX1632 auto-scan mode automatically sequences through selected channels: Configuration steps: Select channels to scan via CHSEL register. Set scan mode bit in control register. Set sampling rate. Start conversion. Operation: ADC automatically switches to next channel after each conversion. DRDY signals when each conversion is complete. Read data via SPI. Sequence repeats continuously. Benefits: Reduces MCU overhead - no need to change channels manually. Ensures consistent timing between channels. Simplifies software design. Example sequence for channels 0-3: Configure CHSEL = 0x0F (channels 0-3 enabled). Set SCAN = 1. Start conversions. DRDY goes low when CH0 data ready. Read CH0 data. DRDY goes low when CH1 data ready. Read CH1 data. Repeat for CH2, CH3, then back to CH0. The auto-scan mode is ideal for data acquisition systems requiring regular sampling of multiple channels.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use auto-scan for continuous multi-channel sampling. Contact our FAE team for scan mode configuration.

faqs.question: What is the power consumption of LX1632 at different sampling rates?
faqs.answer: The LX1632 power consumption scales with sampling rate: Power specifications: 100kSPS: ~10mW (3.3V supply, ~3mA). 50kSPS: ~6mW (~1.8mA). 10kSPS: ~2mW (~0.6mA). Power-down mode: ~10μW (~3μA). Power saving strategies: Use power-down between conversions for low duty cycle. Reduce sampling rate if full speed not needed. Disable unused channels. Use external reference only when needed. Power breakdown: Analog front-end: ~40%. ADC core: ~35%. Digital interface: ~25%. For battery-powered applications: Calculate average power based on duty cycle. Consider trade-off between sampling rate and power. Use burst sampling if continuous monitoring not needed. The LX1632 offers good power efficiency for its performance class.
faqs.decisionGuide: Power scales with sampling rate. Use power-down for low duty cycle. Contact our FAE team for power optimization.

faqs.question: How do I synchronize multiple LX1632 ADCs?
faqs.answer: Synchronizing multiple LX1632 ADCs requires careful timing control: Methods for synchronization: Common CONVST signal: Connect CONVST pins of all ADCs together. Drive with single MCU GPIO. All ADCs start conversion simultaneously. SPI readback sequential. Common clock source: Use external clock for all ADCs. Ensures identical sampling instants. Requires external oscillator distribution. Triggered sampling: Use MCU timer to trigger conversions. Software-controlled synchronization. Less precise but simpler. Best practices: Use same reference voltage for all ADCs. Match PCB trace lengths for CONVST and clock. Consider using ADCs with built-in synchronization. For multi-channel systems: Use one LX1632 with 8 channels when possible. Use multiple ADCs only when needed for higher channel count or isolation. The LX1632 does not have dedicated synchronization pins, so external control is needed for precise synchronization.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use common CONVST for synchronization. Contact our FAE team for multi-ADC system design.

faqs.question: What anti-aliasing filter do I need for LX1632?
faqs.answer: Anti-aliasing filter requirements for the LX1632: Nyquist criterion: Input signals above fs/2 will alias into the passband. For 100kSPS, Nyquist frequency is 50kHz. Filter requirements: Attenuate signals above 50kHz to below 1 LSB. Typical requirement: 60-80dB attenuation at fs/2. Filter options: Single-pole RC: Simple, -20dB/decade. May need high cutoff for adequate attenuation. Multi-pole active filter: Better attenuation, more complex. Switched-capacitor filter: Precise, programmable. Recommended approach: Set filter cutoff at 1/5 to 1/10 of sampling rate (10-20kHz for 100kSPS). Use at least 2-pole filter for 40dB/decade rolloff. Consider input signal bandwidth requirements. Example: 2-pole Butterworth, 10kHz cutoff. Provides 40dB attenuation at 50kHz. Good compromise between bandwidth and aliasing protection. The LX1632 has no internal anti-aliasing filter, so external filtering is required.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 2-pole filter with cutoff at 1/5 to 1/10 of sampling rate. Contact our FAE team for filter design.
